data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: Is Elevidys A Bust?
data.description:
It has been roughly seven months since my previous Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) article titled “Sarepta Therapeutics: Rebuilding the Thesis After Hitting Rock Bottom.” At that time, SRPT was in collapse after ELEVIDYS had become a regulatory nightmare as reports of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) patients dying following treatment. ELEVIDYS went from being viewed as a once-in-a-generation franchise treatment to potentially a safety nightmare that looked like it was destined to be pulled from the market by regulators. Although I did have my concerns, I stressed that it looked like it was only going to be non-ambulatory patients that were going to be pulled from treatment, which would hurt their peak sales estimates, but the market was dismissing the long-term value of Sarepta’s Duchenne franchise. As a result, I believed SRPT was trading at a discount and decided to add to the position in the $15 handle in early August.

Seven months later, we now see that ELEVIDYS was not withdrawn but has black-box warnings, and Sarepta will likely have to deal with payer scrutiny, as well as lingering safety concerns. However, the main concern is that we are seeing a quarter-over-quarter decrease in ELEVIDYS sales. Indeed, the company just reported that their preliminary Q4 and 2025 numbers showed nearly $900M of ELEVIDYS revenue in 2025, pulling in roughly $1.86B in total product sales. Plus, they ended 2025 with almost $1B in cash. However, the market is obviously apprehensive about the fading ELEVIDYS sales and the Q4 “miss.”

It looks as if the same tension that has dictated SRPT for the past several years is still in control of the price action. The market has made a game out of constantly repricing SRPT based on headlines, which has provided plenty of trading opportunities for investors to manage their position around earnings reports, safety headlines, and commercial updates. On the downside, it makes SRPT a laborious investment as investors struggle to maintain long-term revenue models and remain vigilant for company updates. Well, the preliminary Q4/2025 numbers report could be another opportunity as the share price goes into another tailspin as the market attempts to analyze the “miss” and how ELEVIDYS will perform in the coming quarters.

I intend to provide some background on how Sarepta has become a franchise stock and how the fate of SRPT is tied to ELEVIDYS. Then, I will take a look at the preliminary numbers and will discuss how the “miss” on earnings might have investors wondering if ELEVIDYS is a bust. In addition, I will point out some bullish points from Sarepta’s presentation at the JPMorgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference. Plus, I will revisit my leading downside risk and how I plan to manage my SRPT position as we head into 2026.

The Drug Stock

I would claim that Sarepta’s stock over the past several years has been a result of investor confidence in the resilience of the company’s revenue base and the company’s R&D prowess in rare diseases, mainly DMD. The company’s earnings have evolved with their journey to becoming a DMD franchise company to what is now a company dependent on ELEVIDYS for explosive growth.

Indeed, the ticker was trading at a premium with only a fragmented exon-skipping DMD market, which was essentially overlaid by the dawdling, incremental growth of their PMO drugs. That dynamic created a rough environment for SRPT that would rally on positive clinical updates, but the gains would often fade as the Street questioned how large the business was really growing from the PMO therapies. ELEVIDYS changed that with early commercial uptake smashing through the company’s previous projected ceiling. ELEVIDYS was expected to be an option for most DMD patients, which would model a multibillion-dollar opportunity. That opportunity made SRPT transition from a DMD franchise stock to essentially an ELEVIDYS stock.

The volatility since then has simply reflected the market’s attempt to price that future correctly. So, when data, guidance, or commercial updates are positive, the stock moves substantially higher, but when safety concerns, manufacturing questions, or reimbursement issues pop up, or when sales start to slip, the stock drops. However, I must reiterate, the swings are not about whether Sarepta survives; they are more about the market’s prediction of ELEVIDYS.

Concerning Trends

Digging into Sarepta’s reported preliminary numbers, their full-year product revenue was $1.86B, with ELEVIDYS contributing $898.7M in its first full commercial year, while the PMOs combined for $965.6M. So, almost a 50/50 split in revenue for ELEVIDYS and the PMOs. However, when we look at the Q4 numbers, the product revenue was $369.6M, with ELEVIDYS contributing $110.4M, which means the PMOs collected $259.2M. So, the company’s revenue is now 30% ELEVIDYS.

Some have proposed that the SRPT sell-off was because analyst expectations had ELEVIDYS bringing in $130M to $150M in revenue for Q4, but they only recorded $110M, and investors were concerned about the “miss” on expectations. However, Sarepta publicized that six infusions were pushed from late December into early January due to the flu season. ELEVIDYS is $3M per infusion, so that is about $18M of deferred revenue going from Q4 of 2025 to Q1 of 2026. I’m not saying that a miss on the Street’s expectations was not a factor in the sell-off, but I think the commercial trends we are seeing from ELEVIDYS are probably a bigger factor.

Below, we have ELEVIDYS' quarterly numbers for 2025.

Q1: ~$375M

Q2: ~$282M

Q3: ~$131.5M

Q4: $110.4M

Clearly, we have to concede that the decrease in revenue is solely due to a drop in demand. We also have to consider that the Q1 to Q2 drop has to do with some normalization after a very strong Q1, which benefited from rapid early uptake. The Q2 to Q3 drop is likely due to the pause on non-ambulatory treatment and ongoing safety concerns. And the drop from Q3 to Q4 was attributed to flu season and safety-related rescheduling, rather than just pure erosion in ELEVIDYS demand.

Regardless of the reasons why, the market is not going to respond positively to a highly touted drug that has seen an astounding first quarter of launch and has only seen those numbers drop off in less than a year.

Is ELEVIDYS A Bust?

Despite the fading sales numbers, I think it is very hard to argue that ELEVIDYS is a bust when it brought in nearly $900M in first-year revenue, which is exceptional for a gene therapy. Yes, we have to revise our expectations for the near term, but Sarepta’s management reiterated a $500M annual revenue floor for ELEVIDYS, and they expect to exceed it. Although the preliminary earnings numbers are concerning, I don’t think it is absurd to expect normalization in the ambulatory population to improve enough to reach or exceed that $500M level.

Yes, we have to accept that the limitation to the ambulatory population has certainly limited peak sales for the time being, but a steady reversal to quarter-over-quarter growth could revitalize the bullish outlook for ELEVIDYS and SRPT.

I think Sarepta could rally investors if ELEVIDYS could hit these quarterly marks.

Q1: $130–150M

Q2: $160–180M

Q3: $180–200M

Q4: $200–230M

I know that I’m proposing a scenario that is well above the company’s estimated floor for ELEVIDY, but I do believe the company is going to easily surpass $500M and show clear upward momentum to get the market to firmly back SRPT. I must highlight that the company claims that around 80% of the ambulatory DMD remains untreated, so the company could still report strong growth with their current label.

Thankfully, the company has not given up on the non-ambulatory population despite the FDA’s revised indication. Sarepta is working on improved safety protocols to treat non-ambulatory patients again. The company has received the green light from the FDA to initiate Cohort 8 of the ENDEAVOR study, which is trying a boosted immunosuppressive treatment for non-ambulatory DMD patients for ELEVIDYS. Approximately 25 participants will receive sirolimus before and after gene therapy infusion to mitigate the risk of acute liver injury. The data would be expected in the second half of this year, but we could see some early safety data before then. Obviously, if the data is positive, we could see the FDA support resuming commercial dosing of ELEVIDYS in the non-ambulatory population towards the end of 2026 or 2027. Now, I must stress that estimates for the percentage of the U.S. DMD population that is ambulatory vs. non-ambulatory are inconsistent, with ranges going from around 1/3 to 1/2. So, getting the non-ambulatory patient population back could roughly double the addressable market in a year.

So, I would say ELEVIDYS is far from being a bust, with a strong first year, a possible resurgence in year two, and an acceleration in year three, which could help put the therapy back on track to a multi-billion dollar peak sales.

Bullish Highlights Beyond ELEVIDYS

While ELEVIDYS is understandably going to determine how investors treat SRPT, I must stress that Sarepta’s fundamentals remain strong. The company’s PMO franchise is still a remarkably underappreciated foundation that is still growing in value, but also in stability. Keep in mind, EXONDYS, VYONDYS, and AMONDYS together brought in nearly $1B in revenue in 2025. So, ELEVIDYS might not experience a huge inflection this year, but investors should feel reassured that the company’s PMOs have what it takes to maintain cash flow until the company unlocks the non-ambulatory population again.

I also need to highlight Sarepta’s financial positioning, which is roughly $954M in cash and investments and no near-term debt. So, I believe the company has the balance sheet and sustained cash flow to fund their siRNA pipeline.

My point is that Sarepta is at the stage where investors should see them as more than a single drug or therapy story. Admittedly, I don’t think the market will see it that way and will take SRPT where the ELEVIDYS outlook is pointing. However, fundamentally, Sarepta is now a self-funding rare-disease platform with a next-gen pipeline and has the cash position to exercise some strategic optionality… with or without ELEVIDYS hitting its estimated peak sales.

Risk Remains

Despite all my points above… I have to reiterate that SRPT is handcuffed to ELEVIDY for the foreseeable future, which will likely attract elevated volatility. Investors must accept that there is a possibility that sales numbers for ELEVIDYS could get worse before they get better. It is possible that the safety concerns will continue to linger, and patients or providers don’t want to risk the therapy. It is also possible that the non-ambulatory study fails, and that population will never be put back on the label. Even if ELEVIDYS gets back to full strength, we have to accept that the company is treating an incredibly fragile patient population, so adverse effects and deaths might be a permanent risk to any of the company’s therapies. Plus, you also have to consider the manufacturing of these gene therapies and the risk that payers become hesitant to continue supporting these therapies with multi-million dollar price tags.

However, in the face of these risks, I’m keeping my SRPT conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” portfolio.

My Game Plan

My SRPT approach will largely remain the same but will still demand a high degree of focus, discipline, and flexibility as we move deeper into 2026. I was successful in adding to the position in August, around $15 per share, which made me rethink placing small, incremental buy orders around the $25 level.

Now, I’m going to wait and see if the market wants to bring SRPT back down to $18 per share at some point in January, then $19 in February, and then $20 in March. If so, I will make a small addition for the first time in 2026. On the other hand, if the market starts to push SRPT higher, I will wait for the share price to climb above the anchored-VWAP from the June 2024-high before clicking the buy button.

In the end, the overall strategy remains unchanged: to actively trade SRPT to generate profits while collecting a long-term “house money” position that is funded by the market.

data.title: ADX: 8% Yield On Large Caps
data.description:
Looking for high-yield income from diversified large-cap stocks?

The Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) is one of the oldest closed-end funds, "CEF's," in the US, having begun in 1929. It has been paying dividends since 1929.

Fund Profile:

"Adams Diversified Equity Fund, a closed-end fund, seeks to deliver superior returns over time by investing in a broadly diversified equity portfolio. The fund invests in a blend of high-quality, large-cap companies. The fund seeks to generate returns that exceed its benchmark as well as consistently distribute dividend income and capital gains to shareholders." (ADX site)

ADX is in the top 3 largest market cap equity CEFs, with $3.04B in Net Assets. It holds 94 positions, with an average daily volume of 302K, and no leverage.

Its annual expense ratio is just 0.50%, with no leverage.

Holdings:

Tech is by far the largest sector exposure, at 34.5%, followed distantly by Financials, 13%; Consumer Discretionary, at 10.7%; Communications Services, at 10.1%; Healthcare, at 9%; and Industrials, at 7.8%; and Consumer Staples, at 5%. The remaining sector exposures run from 1.6% to 3.2%:

As of 9/30/25, ADX's top 10 holdings included all the Magnificent 7 stocks, in addition to Broadcom, Chase, and Adams Natural Resources Fund, which gave ADX exposure to energy stocks. The top 10 positions accounted for ~43% of ADX's portfolio.

Dividends:

At its 1/14/26 closing price of $23.10, ADX yielded 7.97% - it pays a quarterly $0.46 distribution.

It has a very high 5-year distribution growth rate of 42.61%, due to its 2021 payouts jumping 189%, from $1.03 to $2.98, and its 2024 payouts rising 92%, from $1.30 to $2.50.

For the 6 months ended 6/30/25, annualized NII rose ~10% vs. full year 2024, whereas annualized Net Realized Gains fell 36%. NII/Gains Distribution coverage was .96X for Q1-2 '25, vs. 1.12X in 2024.

Annualized Unrealized Appreciation for the 6 months ended 6/30/26 was down 19.6%.

Net Assets rose $112M in full year 2024 and $142M in the 6 months ended 6/30/26:

Taxes:

Management estimated that 2025 distributions were comprised of 85% Long Term Gains, 9% NII, and 6% in Short Term Gains:

Performance:

Alphabet (GOOG), Broadcom (AVGO), NVIDIA (NVDA), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) led ADX's top 10 holdings over the past year, with price gains of ~75%, ~51%, 37.5%, and 26%, respectively.

ADX gained 13.3% in price, with a total return of ~21%, outperforming the S&P 500's 19.79% total return. AAPL, MSFT, TSLA, AMZN, META, and PEO were the top 10's lowest performers over the past year.

As for the start of 2026, Alphabet continues to outperform, rising ~7%, and PEO is up 7.2%, whereas the rest of ADX's top 10 are in the red, with ADX itself down ~1%, vs. the S&P's 1.2% gain:

Longer term, ADX has 3-, 5-, and 10-year NAV returns of 23%, 15%, and 15.5%, respectively, with somewhat higher price returns of 28.3%, 17%, and 16.6%:

ADX outperformed the Morningstar US CEF US Equity category on a price and NAV return basis in 2023-2025, 2018-2020, and in 2021 on an NAV basis:

Risks:

Interest Rate Risk: As seen in the above table, ADX can be negatively impacted by rising interest rates - it fell nearly 20% in 2022, a year of rising rates.

Valuations:

CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion.

ADX's NAV valuation of -6.86% is not nearly as deep of a discount as its 1-, 3-, and 5-year valuations of -8.56%, -11.79%, and -12.81%.

Parting Thoughts:

While ADX has a strong record of outperformance, its current discount is not that attractive. We advise putting it on your watchlist and waiting for a deeper discount before adding new shares.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: The Setup Looks Easy, But It Isn't (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Kinder Morgan's Earnings Approach

Kinder Morgan (KMI) offers us exposure to key megatrends. I have been discussing natural gas and LNG over and over. Since it is a midstream operator, it is linked with two trends: domestic demand for natural gas is rising as a consequence of data centers and the manufacturing reshoring; LNG exports from the Gulf Coast to Europe and Asia. Its business model is strong because around 90% of its revenue is not directly linked to commodity prices, either coming from take-or-pay contracts or fees on volumes. As a result, the stock falls into the tollbooth category thanks to its steady cash flows. Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream operators in the U.S., since it moves around 40% of domestic gas. It usually reports EBITDA margins above 40%, which the company has wisely used to reduce its leverage to around 3.8x, which is acceptable for midstream. Two quarters ago, I initiated my KMI coverage with a neutral rating since I thought the company was already priced correctly when we looked at its multiples. It seems the market agreed, as the stock has been trading sideways since then, moving between $26 and $29.

As Kinder Morgan's Q4 2025 earnings approach, we have an interesting opportunity to update our research and see what we could be expecting from the company and whether we are before an opportunity to buy the stock at a reasonable price or not.

Kinder Morgan's Earnings Preview

Kinder Morgan's Q3 was a strong report, with EBITDA up 6% and adj. EPS up 16% YoY. The company confirmed that the natural gas business was outperforming the other businesses, accounting for over two-thirds of the overall budget.

By now, we know that Q4 is also expected to be strong. The consensus expects revenue growth of 8.6% and EPS growth above 14%. For midstream, these are fast-growing rates. So, unless an unexpected print is released, I don't think this will move the needle. As long as gas transport volumes grow above 4% and the leverage ratio stays below 4x, I don't think we will see a big reaction in either direction.

What matters about this report is the 2026 outlook. To this regard, Kinder Morgan issued a note in December where it said that it expects its FY26 EBITDA to grow 4% more than what it had forecasted at the end of Q3. In other words, a little over a month after it released its Q3 earnings, it had to increase its guidance by 4 full percentage points. This is a big change, and it confirms that Q4 should be strong. In addition to the quarterly changes, we also know that KMI has committed more than $9B to expansion projects (two years ago, it was at $3B), which shows how real the gas demand surge is.

All estimates believe that by 2030, natural gas demand will reach 28 Bcf/day, a 21.4% increase versus 2024. This is sustained growth for a commodity such as gas. This leads us to capex and interest rates. Most of us believe that this year we will see interest rates go down, either moderately or steeply. This will be an advantage for a company such as KMI because it will lower the cost of its debt and its interest expense. Just to give you an idea of how much its leverage can weigh on it, a 0.1x reduction equals around $850M. This is why KMI has indeed been growing its dividend but has been opportunistic with its buyback policy, choosing to reduce its leverage before committing to big share repurchase programs.

Kinder Morgan's Valuation

Overall, I like the company's business; I like its exposure to natural gas and LNG; I like its domestic footprint and the assets it operates. I think for several years it could truly perform well. This positions the company as an interesting stock to own. Let's then look at the valuation multiples to see whether it is a buy or not ahead of the earnings release after the close on Wednesday, January 21.

Right now, KMI trades at a fwd P/E of 21.4, a fwd EV/EBITDA of 11.4, and a fwd P/FCF of 10.4. The dividend yield is 4.25%, and the FCF yield is 4.5%. In a certain way, these multiples make the stock appear as a bond-proxy. The multiples are not too expensive, but they are not cheap either. Its cash flows behave similarly to bonds, but there are some differences. For example, when rates move down, this inflates KMI's stock price, whereas bonds usually lose value. Secondly, KMI has an underlying business that can grow and can report surprises. More or less, these multiples tell us that investors are confident about KMI's current cash flows and their moderate growth, but don't expect a rapid acceleration. This makes sense, since the increase in the project backlog will inevitably impact the company's FCF generation. In this scenario, small beats don't matter, while misses can trigger a rerating if they are a consequence of altered costs or leverage. It is a setup where good news appears but stops being enough to drive the stock up and out of the range that has caught it in recent months.

Right now, I think the chance for KMI to hold its value and keep paying dividends is high because the setup I described points in that direction. However, it is not the ideal situation for a new investor to jump aboard. It also doesn't look like a momentum trade, as this earnings report seems to me more a confirmation quarter, rather than a make-or-break moment. After all, right now Treasuries offer the same yield and (probably) a similar stability. KMI doesn't seem to be poised to exit from its current trading range unless a new catalyst appears (i.e., big interest rate cuts). As much as I like the company, my rating on the stock is still neutral.

data.title: J.M. Smucker: Buy This Dividend Aristocrat While It's On Sale
data.description:
Successful investing doesn’t have to be a popularity contest. Instead of chasing growth, I’m more content with pursuing value stocks that are unpopular with the market today so that I can get bigger gains in the future. In the words of football great Jerry Rice: “Today I will do what others won’t, so tomorrow I can do what others can’t.” What’s even better is if the value stock pays you to wait with a decent dividend income stream.

This brings me to J.M. Smucker (SJM), which I last covered in October, highlighting its growing key brands, innovation, and expanding market reach. SJM remains in value range at the current price of $104.78. As shown below, it sits in the bottom half of its 52-week range with a forward P/E of 11.6 and a 4.2% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit SJM, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s a value and income "Buy" at present for potentially strong total returns, so let’s dig in!

Why SJM?

J.M. Smucker is a consumer staples giant that serves markets across North America. It has a leading product portfolio that covers coffee, peanut butter, fruit spreads, frozen handhelds, sweet baked goods, and dog and cat food. Its notable brands include Folgers, Dunkin, Café Bustelo, Jif, Uncrustables, Smucker’s, Hostess, Milk-Bone, and Meow Mix.

It’s fairly well known by now that consumer staples names like General Mills (GIS), Campbell’s Company (CPB), and Hormel (HRL) have faced margin pressures due to higher costs of goods and price sensitivity among consumers. SJM is no different, with operating margin falling from the +18% level to 16.5% over the trailing 12 months. Nonetheless, SJM’s operating margin still sits above the 12% level from 2023, and revenue has remained stable after growing materially in 2024, as shown below.

SJM produced decent top-line growth during fiscal Q2 2026 (ended Oct. 31, 2025) with net sales rising by 3% YoY to $2.3 billion. Excluding divestitures and foreign currency exchange, comparable net sales grew by a more appealing 5% over the prior year period. Sales growth was driven by an 11 percentage point increase in prices, particularly for strong growing brands like Café Bustelo. This more than offset a 6 percentage point decrease in volume in products, as shown below, driven by lower coffee, peanut butter, and dog snacks volume.

At the same time, higher commodity costs and an unfavorable product mix (more sales of lower-margin products) resulted in a gross profit decline of 2 percent. Despite challenges in volume during fiscal Q2, SJM’s market share has been improving, particularly in Sweet Baked Snacks and the Hostess brand (acquired in late 2023). SJM is also seeing sequential improvement in the convenience store channel. This is driven by portfolio simplification, in which 25% of SKUs were eliminated in favor of focusing on core brands like SJM’s market-leading Donuts, Donettes, and Cupcakes.

Looking ahead, SJM is positioning itself to stabilize demand and grow margins through higher brand investment this year. At the same time, management continues to streamline operations, including the planned closure of the Indianapolis facility by fiscal Q4, which is expected to save $30 million in annual run rate costs. SJM is also unlocking value through brand synergies by introducing Peanut Butter Bites to Milk-Bone brand through a collaboration with Jif.

Importantly, the fast-growing Uncrustables brand continues to be a growth driver for the company. This is reflected by 7% sales growth in the Away From Home category (including Uncrustables). SJM intends to grow Uncrustables to be a $1 billion brand with new recent offerings, including peanut butter and chocolate called PB Choco Craze, and two new higher protein items in the product pipeline. Notably, the U.S. protein market is expected to grow at a robust 8.8% CAGR through 2034 to $6.1 billion, as shown below.

SJM is currently moderately leveraged after its recent acquisitions, including Hostess Brands. Management expects the net debt-to-leverage ratio to be around 4x for the balance of fiscal 2026. With debt paydown, they expect leverage to step down toward the safer 3x level in fiscal 2027. This appears to be a credible plan to ratings agency S&P as SJM maintains a BBB credit rating.

Importantly for income investors, SJM currently yields 4.2%, and the dividend is well-covered by a 49% payout ratio, leaving plenty of room for debt paydown and brand portfolio investments. Notably, SJM is a Dividend Aristocrat with 28 consecutive years of raises under its belt and a 5-year dividend CAGR of 4.1%. As shown below, SJM’s dividend yield currently sits at the high end of its 10-year range.

I see value in SJM at the current price of $104.78 with a forward P/E of 11.6, which, as shown below, sits far below its historical P/E of 15.9. SJM’s valuation also sits well under the consumer staples sector median P/E, which also happens to be 15.9.

At the current valuation, I believe the market is too bearish around SJM’s recent headwinds and margin pressures. The profitability issue may stabilize over the course of this year through the aforementioned portfolio and process simplification. At the same time, growth brands like Café Bustelo and Uncrustables should continue to drive growth for SJM. Analysts estimate 7% to 9% annual EPS growth over the next 2 fiscal years. This, combined with the 4.2% dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, could result in total annual returns in the mid-teens.

Risks to SJM include its higher leverage ratio at present while it also plans to invest in R&D and marketing. This exposes SJM to risks from interest rates should they go higher. SJM also has commodity price risks for products such as coffee, which are subject to weather, tariffs, and/or geopolitical tensions, which could eat into margins. Plus, changing consumer tastes and preferences away from packaged foods and towards healthier products could result in pressure on SJM’s brands.

Investor Takeaway

J.M. Smucker offers a classic value-and-income setup at present. SJM trades near the low end of its historical valuation, despite steady demand for its core brands and clear plans to stabilize margins. Near-term headwinds from commodity costs, mix pressure, and higher leverage remain. However, these risks appear to be more than reflected in the current multiple.

At the same time, SJM’s portfolio simplification, cost savings, and growth drivers like Uncrustables and Café Bustelo provide a path to earnings recovery. Investors are paid to wait with a well-covered 4.2% dividend backed by a long track record of annual increases. As such, I maintain a "Buy" rating on SJM for potentially strong total returns from its current undervaluation.

data.title: Silver Enters Consolidation After Rejecting Highs Above $94
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Silver is trading just under the $91 level on Friday after failing to sustain the upper end of its recent surge, with price action shifting into consolidation rather than signaling a trend reversal. The metal has cooled following sharp intraday swings, but the broader structure remains firmly bullish, with the latest pullback reflecting profit-taking after an outsized rally.

The retreat comes after one of the most aggressive upside moves in years, which pushed silver deep into overextended territory. While volatility has increased and short-term momentum has softened, there is little evidence so far of broad-based distribution or a breakdown in underlying demand. Instead, the price action points to digestion of gains as traders reassess positioning after the rally’s intensity.

Uptrend intact despite short-term exhaustion

On the daily chart, silver continues to trade in a powerful uptrend. Price remains well above all major EMAs, underscoring the strength of the longer-term structure. The 20-day EMA sits near $78.9, the 50-day around $67.9, the 100-day close to $58.6, and the 200-day near $49.5. This wide separation between price and trend indicators confirms that bullish momentum remains dominant on higher timeframes.

Silver price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

The rejection from above $94 has introduced two-sided trade, but it has not materially damaged the trend. After a vertical advance, some degree of retracement or sideways movement is typical as markets rebalance. Even with the pullback, silver remains deeply extended relative to its moving averages, which helps explain the recent increase in intraday volatility.

Momentum indicators support the view that the market is cooling rather than turning lower. Daily RSI has eased from overbought levels but remains elevated in the low 70s. Crucially, there is no clear bearish divergence, suggesting that momentum is moderating without signaling a reversal. Historically, similar RSI behavior has often accompanied consolidation phases following sharp rallies, particularly when prices have advanced too quickly.

Short-term reset as catalysts fade

Shorter timeframes highlight the ongoing reset. On the 30-minute chart, silver has slipped below its intraday Supertrend and is oscillating between roughly $89 and $92. Parabolic SAR dots remain above price, indicating that sellers have near-term control. However, downside follow-through has been limited, with buyers stepping in quickly on dips toward the $89 to $90 area.

This behavior points to range-building rather than aggressive liquidation. The $94 to $95 zone now stands out as clear resistance, marking the ceiling that capped the latest advance. A sustained move back above that region would likely signal trend continuation and reopen the door to further upside extension. On the downside, first support lies around $88 to $89, with a deeper pullback toward $84 to $85 still considered technically healthy given how far price has run.

Fundamentals help explain both the rally and the subsequent pause. Silver’s surge was driven in part by fears of potential U.S. tariffs on critical minerals, which prompted aggressive front-loading of shipments and pushed prices to record levels. News that those tariffs would be delayed removed a key short-term catalyst, encouraging profit-taking and a reset in positioning.

Even so, broader macro forces continue to underpin the market. Safe-haven demand tied to geopolitical tensions, along with persistent concerns about Federal Reserve independence and longer-term inflation risks, has kept investor interest in precious metals elevated. Those factors have helped silver hold onto the bulk of its gains, leaving it on pace for a weekly advance of more than 13% despite the late pullback.

Market outlook

Silver now appears to be consolidating excess rather than losing its trend. Near-term price action is likely to remain choppy as the market digests recent gains and adjusts to the removal of short-term catalysts. As long as silver holds above the high-$80s, the medium-term bullish structure remains intact. A break back above $94 would signal renewed momentum, while a deeper retracement toward the mid-$80s would still fit within a healthy correction.

Previously, we noted that silver’s rapid ascent left it vulnerable to volatility as prices moved far ahead of underlying trend measures. That view is now playing out, with consolidation replacing acceleration. So far, the market response supports the idea that this phase represents digestion within strength rather than the start of a broader reversal.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Goodbye Free Money, Hello Dividends
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Not so long ago, investors could earn over 5% yields on cash sitting in money market funds or high-yield savings accounts.

Today, that has fallen to about 4%.

And as the Federal Reserve cuts their key policy rate again a few more times in 2026, as they are widely expected to do, yields on cash-equivalent vehicles will likely fall to about 3%.

The trajectory of the Fed Funds Rate is one direction right now: Down.

And to add insult to injury, inflation is sticking stubbornly around 3%.

As the yields on cash-equivalent vehicles trend down toward 3% and inflation stays sticky at around that level, the gap between the two is closing.

In other words, the past few years of "free money" (in the sense of cash yields higher than inflation) are coming to an end.

What can income-seeking investors with sizable cash positions do to prevent income erosion as cash-equivalent yields fall?

Below, we offer three ideas for how investors can reposition their cash to keep their income stable or growing.

Three Ways to Prevent Yield Erosion

We hate to break it to you, but just as there is no free lunch in economics, there is also no free lunch in investing.

As ultra-low-risk cash-equivalent yields decline, there is nowhere one can go to increase yield without taking additional risk. It's better to come to peace with that than to be lured into some huckster's promise of "low-risk high-yields."

And make no mistake: Cash vehicle yields are falling. They will keep falling with each Fed rate cut.

Take a look at the trailing twelve-month yields of these popular ultra-short-term fixed-income ETFs:

  • PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT)
  • Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB)
  • iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH)
  • JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST)
  • State Street SPDR 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL)
  • F/m US Treasury 3 Month Bill ETF (TBIL)

Trailing twelve-month yields average all monthly payments over the last year together, but obviously this includes a period in which the Fed Funds Rate was higher than it is today.

If you look at 30-day SEC yields, which do a better job at measuring current yields, all of the above vehicles are down to about 3.75% to a little over 4%. As short-term securities mature and are replaced with lower-yielding, newly issued securities, those yields will keep edging down.

And yet, there is an absolutely massive (and growing!) amount of capital sitting in these cash-equivalent funds.

This doesn't even count the huge amount of capital invested in ultra-short-term bond funds like the ones listed above!

We have observed in past interest rate cycles that it takes upward of a few years into a rate-cutting cycle for investors to begin diverting funds from cash-equivalent vehicles into other areas of the market. Many investors notice their cash savings generating less income only slowly over time.

But after a few years have passed and the Fed Funds Rate has reached a low level, say 3% or less, many of the alternative yield investments have already been bid up.

That's why we think it is better for cash-heavy investors to get ahead of the curve and begin repositioning their cash before other investors are doing the same en masse.

So what can income-oriented investors do to mitigate the loss of yield from their cash positions?

There are three typical options.

Option 1: Go Longer in Duration With Individual Bonds or CDs

One option that will appeal to some investors is to lock in a certain yield for a set number of years by buying individual bonds or certificates of deposit ("CDs").

Even after interest rates have come down this year, one can still buy a 5-7 year Treasury bond or CD with anywhere between a 3.6% to 3.9% yield.

It may look like someone invested in a money market fund or ultra-short-term ETF is giving up some yield to invest in a 5- or 7-year bond, but that is merely an illusion based on trailing twelve-month yields. If you estimate the next twelve-month yields for those cash-equivalent vehicles, you'd probably end up somewhere in the low- to mid-3% territory. So, in reality, one likely isn't giving up much yield over the next year to make such a trade, and one is also locking in that income stream for a long period of time.

However, there are significant downsides to investing in individual bonds or CDs.

Locking in your money for that long of a timespan takes away a lot of optionality. You might want or need that money at some point during that time period, which opens the possibility of selling the bond or CD at a loss and eliminating some or all returns you see from it.

Bonds, even ones as short in duration as 5 years, are not ideal vehicles for dry powder or emergency funds. Unless you know exactly when you will need the funds in the future, investing in individual bonds often isn't worth the additional risk.

Option 2: High-Grade Preferred Stocks

Again, we proffer the reminder that it is impossible to increase yield without, to some degree, going up the risk curve.

While the market may not be perfectly efficient, it isn't stupid. Yield is almost always an efficient signal of risk.

That said, if one is going to increase risk to capture additional yield, we think a good way to do that would be through investment-grade-rated preferred stocks.

Preferreds sit between common equity and debt/bonds in the capital hierarchy, meaning that claims on company cash are highest for bondholders, then for preferred stockholders, then for common stockholders.

Preferreds generally pay higher yields than either bonds or common stocks. They are basically fixed-income securities, which makes them an interesting candidate as an alternative investment for cash.

But given preferreds' lower ranking in the capital stack, it is important to stick to higher-quality securities. The last thing an investor wants to do when allocating some cash to an alternative yield investment is to endure significant volatility or dividend/distribution cuts down the road.

That's why the preferred stocks of investment-grade corporations make a compelling option.

Consider, for example, the Innovator S&P Investment Grade Preferred ETF (EPRF), which charges 47 basis points in fees as the only pure-play IG preferred stock ETF on the market (as of this writing, at least). Its 70-something holdings are mostly issued by companies in the financial sector with a minority in the real estate sector. EPRF yields about 5.5%, and about 3/4ths of the ETF's distributions are classified as qualified dividends.

In terms of income safety, IG-rated preferred stocks are an attractive replacement for cash-equivalent vehicles.

But during market crashes, even IG preferreds can and do sell off along with the rest of the stock market, albeit somewhat less so.

During the COVID-19 selloff, EPRF dropped about 25% in value, while the S&P 500 (SPY) sold off about 35%.

To the extent that a cash position is intended to be a stable source of dry powder to use precisely during such selloffs, investment-grade preferreds are not necessarily the best option.

But if one intends to allocate only a portion of one's cash position to IG preferreds to increase yield, we think EPRF is an interesting option to consider.

There are also a handful of IG-rated preferred stocks that look attractive today. Consider just two of them:

Yield Upside to Par
Rexford Industrial Realty Series C (REXR.PR.C) 6.6% 18%
Regency Centers Series B (REGCO) 6.5% 12%

REXR.PR.C is issued by Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), a high-quality multi-tenant industrial REIT concentrated entirely in the Southern California region, where there is extremely little available land for new construction.

REGCO is issued by Regency Centers (REG), a high-quality grocery-anchored shopping center REIT with a large and nationally diversified portfolio.

The downsides of these two real estate preferreds are that they do not pay qualified dividends, and both have very low trading volume, making it difficult to trade in or out.

Again: There's no free lunch.

Option 3: High-Quality Dividend Stocks

The third and final option we'll consider is to go a little further up the risk curve into the realm of dividend stocks. However, we acknowledge that an income-seeking investor would only consider transferring funds from an ultra-conservative cash-equivalent vehicle into dividend stocks if those stocks were high-quality and boasted safe payouts.

Furthermore, these high-quality dividend stocks would need to be mostly large-cap in size to capture mature businesses that have weathered multiple cycles in the past. And it would help if the lion's share of these dividend stocks operated in defensive industries like consumer staples, healthcare, utilities, and integrated oil & gas.

We can think of three dividend ETFs that fit these criteria:

Yield Plausible Dividend Growth Rate
Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) 3.8% Mid-Single-Digit
VictoryShares US Lg Cap High Div Vol-Weighted ETF (CDL) 3.3% Mid-Single-Digit
First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders ETF (FDL) 4.0% Mid-Single-Digit

Each of these three ETFs has its own unique stock-picking methodology, but each one overwhelmingly holds high-quality, large-cap, defensively tilted stock portfolios.

And each has a solid track record of dividend growth at the fund level, which should give an investor confidence that their income streams will continue to grow in the future.

Again, however, we acknowledge that even the bluest of blue-chip dividend stocks are not ideal replacements for a cash-equivalent vehicle like an ultra-short-term bond ETF or a money market fund. They are more volatile and susceptible to market downturns.

As such, we would envision that smart, income-seeking investors would only devote a portion of their cash positions to high-quality dividend stocks in order to increase yield while keeping some cash as a portfolio stabilizer and source of dry powder.

Bottom Line

We won't lie to you.

There is no alternative to cash-equivalent vehicles that provide higher yields for the same amount of risk. If there were, people would find out about them, and the market would eliminate the arbitrage.

The only way for income-seeking investors to mitigate falling cash yields is by increasing risk to some degree.

But that doesn't mean investors should throw caution to the wind or accept stupid risk. Not at all!

At High Yield Investor, we are highly selective about where we hunt for quality yield and what margins of safety we require when buying them.

We think income investors seeking to maintain their passive income should begin to consider deploying some of their cash into higher-yielding alternatives like individual bonds, high-grade preferred stocks, or high-quality dividend stocks.

The right solution will surely depend on the individual investor, but one thing is certain: The last few years of "free money" from cash-equivalent vehicles is coming to an end.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 254: $25,400 Allocated, $2,720.76 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
2025 is off to a great start as the December CPI numbers came in at 2.7% YoY while Core CPI hovered at 2.6% YoY. This is keeping the soft landing thesis alive and reducing the probability of a tightening cycle resurfacing anytime soon. The headline numbers on retail sales beat expectations as they rose in November by 0.4%. The housing market started to improve as existing home sales increased 5.1% in December. We’re heading into earnings season with a strong macroeconomic setup for corporations. The S&P 500 continues to try to climb past 7,000 and is hovering around all-time highs. I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned well for the future, as many of the companies I have invested in should do well in the new economic environment. I am planning on continuing to allocate capital toward energy, real estate, and other companies that should benefit from a lower rate environment. As the risk-free rate of return declines, I believe that many of the positions within the portfolio will look much more attractive as their yield will more than double what can be generated on the sideline. I am looking forward to continuing this series and documenting every aspect of this journey to showcase that anyone can build an income-producing portfolio from the ground up without a large amount of capital.

After allocating $25,400 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 254 at $33,837.38. The account's profitability increased (2.29%) to $8,437.38, which is a return on invested capital of 33.22%. This week, I added to my positions in ONEOK (OKE) and Verizon (VZ). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $58.81 of dividend income in week 254. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $9.60 (0.35%) to $2,720.76. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,706.16 of income since its inception, which is 22.55% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I believe that we will get an everything rally in 2026 that is met with a large drawdown of 10-15%, but the market will ultimately reach around 8,000. I am excited for 2026, as the combination of a lower rate environment, inflation cooling, and GDP growth should be a winning combination for equities.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I started this account with $100 with the goal of documenting how I would build out an income-producing portfolio from the ground up on a budget of $100 per week. The reality is that this type of investing isn’t for everyone, as there is a large cohort of investors looking to generate as much alpha as possible. While I have many investments for generating capital appreciation, I also have many investments in income-producing assets. I intend to continue this account for another 20 years in addition to my other income investments, with a goal of generating enough income to live off without touching my actual retirement assets. I am now generating $2,720.76 in forward dividend income while being up $8,437.38 on invested capital. This account continues to impress me, and it's serving as a blueprint for what is possible with just $100 per week. Over time, this account will be generating 5 figures of income, and I am excited to see how 2026 unfolds.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $903.27 (33.20%)
  • Equities $632.60 (23.25%)
  • REITs $457.54 (16.82%)
  • CEFs $453.81 (16.68%)
  • BDCs $273.55 (10.05%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself for 30 years in the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. In 2025 I collected $2,411.27 in dividend income from 831 dividends. After 2 weeks in 2026, $159.45 of dividend income was produced, which is 6.61% of the total income from 2025. There have been 22 dividends produced in 2026, which is 2.65% of the dividends generated in 2025.

There was $100.64 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $79.73. This is an increase of $33.35 (117.03%) YoY, which is because 2025 ended and 2026 started in the same week. I'm really excited for the rest of October to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

January is starting out to be a great month. After 7 trading days, there has been $82.49 of dividend income generated from the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. When I think about the current monthly average in dividend income exceeding $225, it’s going to be interesting to see what happens throughout the month. In 2025, January was the largest income-producing month until July rolled around. I have a feeling that I will get close to $250 of dividend income this month. January will set the stage for the rest of the year, and I believe there is a good chance that I can generate close to $3,000 this year in dividend income.

There are currently 41 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There's $295.50 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 39.52% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 23.25% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.48% of the portfolio and generate 76.75% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have sectors close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

It’s crazy that Altria Group (MO) shot up this week as shares rose to $57.53, which put it back to 3.9% of the portfolio. In 2 weeks it will be 2 years since I have allocated capital toward MO, and it's still the number one holding in the portfolio. I think this is a reflection of being able to find value across the market and how diversifying into different assets can help offset positions that haven’t performed as well. The top 20 largest holdings have continued to even out throughout 2025, and I am excited about the fact that the portfolio is getting less top-heavy. I am happy that this has turned out to be a diversified portfolio that can protect me from fluctuations in the market.

The top 10 holdings didn’t change week over week, but several positions generated their dividends. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,773.92, and they finished the week with a value of $9,532.59. This is an ROI of $2,758.67 (40.72%). There has been $1,374.81 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.30% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $677.58 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10%. These positions represent 28.17% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 24.90% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 254 Additions

In week 254, I added to ONEOK (OKE) and Verizon (VZ)

ONEOK (OKE)

  • I believe that OKE’s integration of EnLink and Medallion assets is ahead of schedule, which is driving substantial EBITDA, revenue, and operating income growth with $450M in realized synergies
  • OKE is trading at 15.86x FCF and 6.28x EBITDA, which I believe is extremely undervalued for its growth potential and profitability
  • I am bullish on OKE’s fee-based contracts, multi-year organic projects, and anticipated lower cost of capital because it positions them for capital appreciation and dividend growth
  • I think that OKE is an undervalued gem that yields over 5% in an environment where the risk-free rate of return is declining

Verizon (VZ)

  • I think that VZ trading under $40 is an incredible deal for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio
  • Most people are scared that VZ has $127 billion in long-term debt, but the reality is that they are generating $50.19 billion in EBITDA and have a 2.53x long-term debt to EBITDA ratio
  • VZ is generating $21.12 billion in free cash flow (FCF), and after paying $11.41 billion in dividends, they still have $9.71 billion in FCF to pay down debt
  • I am able to get VZ yielding close to 7% with more than 2 decades of dividend growth under its belt at an EBITDA to long-term debt valuation that makes sense

Week 255 Gameplan

This week I plan on adding to my position in the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) and potentially VZ again if it is under $40

Conclusion

Every time I write this article, I get more bullish for the future. 2026 is off to a great start, and I believe that I will generate close to $250 of dividend income this month from the underlying holdings. This portfolio was started with $100, and after allocating $25,400 over the past 254 weeks, I am generating a portfolio yield of 8.04% ($2,720.76), and I am up $8,437.38 on my invested capital. I believe my thesis will hold up that the combination of lower rates, oil prices between $55-$65, and lower inflation will drive GDP higher and create a macroeconomic environment where the market can rally 10-15% in 2026. I believe the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned well for the future, and I am excited to continue allocating capital each week toward this series. Please leave all of your comments and suggestions below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone.

data.title: AI + America + SCHG = Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Introduction

With the rapid adoption of artificial intelligence, I believe AI will continue to reshape America for many years to come. Not only America, but on a global scale as well.

As someone who fought for this country for 21 years after serving in the military, I can say I'm excited to see where things will go.

I expect 2026 to be a great year for the overall market as AI CAPEX spending will likely continue being a growth driver, allowing the market to again see double-digit returns.

One way I'm investing in the revolution is through the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

In this article, I discuss the growth expectations of AI and why SCHG is a compelling choice despite risks.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Schwab's U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF back in October, assigning them a buy rating in an article titled: A Simple, Low-Cost Fund For Big Growth And Long-Term Power.

Since the ETF is up over 1%, in comparison to the S&P (SP500) up closer to 3%. Aside from their high concentration in technology, SCHG's strong NAV growth, low-cost structure, and qualified distributions made them a compelling investment long-term.

Making America Great Again

No matter your political stance, President Trump seems to be making good on his promise to make the country great. Aside from issuing several AI-related executive orders, our invasion of Venezuela will likely benefit us long-term.

With Chevron (CVX) being the only U.S. Energy (XLE) company with operations in the country currently, this will also likely drive growth.

Venezuela's heavier crudes vs. America's lighter ones will likely benefit us in the long run, as President Trump has stated plans for the U.S. to sell Venezuela's oil to other countries.

While there's uncertainty currently surrounding how this will turn out, I can't help but imagine this boding well for America down the line. With 2026 off to a fresh start, I believe this year the market will see solid returns.

While it may not see returns above 20% like 2023 and 2024, I think we can see similar returns to last year. At a minimum, I believe we will see returns in the double digits, mostly thanks to AI.

There have also been talks of the President using the military to acquire Greenland for its minerals to strengthen America's national security. And to continue the AI buildout. If successful, this will likely also bode well long-term.

Why SCHG Will Likely Continue Being a Wealth Compounder

There are several high-quality, tech-focused ETFs to choose from currently. However, SCHG is one of my favorites. For starters, the ETF has a long track record of outperformance.

While funds or companies may have had a few good years, I view anything over 5 years as not a fluke. SCHG has a long track record of approximately 16 years with an inception date of 12/11/2009.

This, and their very low-cost structure of just 0.04%, makes them attractive for long-term investors. While higher expense ratios are not an end-all be-all for me, those with higher ratios closer to the 1% level become less attractive.

This is because over time, fees can eat into your returns. While 1% doesn't sound like much, 1% on millions of dollars can add up pretty quickly. My ETFs are my core holdings, meaning I plan to add to them over time, no matter what the market is doing.

SCHG's low-cost structure is due to it being passively managed. But some may view this as a disadvantage; the fund's long-term performance says otherwise.

SCHG rebalances on an annual basis, and Technology currently accounts for 48% of its portfolio. NVIDIA (NVDA) is its top holding, accounting for close to 11%.

NVDA is expected to release its next-generation high-performance computer sometime in the back half of this year. Management stated they expect up to $500 billion in AI demand.

The company also introduced the world's first thinking model to autonomous driving. With this expected to come online in Q1 of this year and the likelihood to see several big-name car companies adopt this, I believe NVDA will continue to dominate the landscape. Thus, benefitting SCHG.

Below is the expected growth of the AI market over the next 9 years, anticipated to reach close to $3.7 trillion.

Aside from NVDA, SCHG's top 10 includes Tesla (TSLA), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT); companies I expect to lead the way in the AI revolution.

Proven Track Record

As previously mentioned, SCHG's track record has been proven year after year. At the time of writing, the ETF has slightly outperformed the S&P (SP500) in the past year.

While some may say SCHG's performance is due to AI enthusiasm, the ETF has outperformed the index over the past three years.

And five years.

At the time of writing, they've also outperformed Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and Vanguard's Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (MGK) over the past 5 years.

In the past 10 years, SCHG's performance has also been solid, beating MGK but lagging QQQ by a sizable amount.

So, from a performance standpoint, I don't believe SCHG's quality can be questioned. And with the share split in October of 2024, this gives investors better access to capture growth. Although stock splits change nothing fundamentally about the fund.

Risks & Conclusion

Over the past 3 months, SCHG has underperformed the index, up 1.5% compared to 2.69%. I think this has mostly to do with the valuation. Currently, SCHG's P/E is significantly higher than the index's at 38.23x.

The S&P currently sits at close to 28x. As a result, SCHG could see underperformance should the market experience a steep pullback.

Moreover, we could see AI optimism fade, and investors could rotate back into safe haven stocks looking for value once interest rates decline and yields on fixed-rate investments become less attractive.

As a result, we could see a prolonged pullback in AI-related stocks, impacting SCHG's performance for the near to medium term. But long-term, investors should look to take advantage and add on any signs of share price weakness.

Over an extended period, I expect SCHG to perform well with returns, at a minimum, in the 10% to 12% range for 2026 due to their heavy concentration in Technology. As a result, I maintain my buy rating on SCHG for long-term investors looking to capture growth from America's AI revolution.

data.title: Incyte's Pipeline Potential Signals Massive Upside For Investors
data.description:
Buying Incyte on Strong Financials and Discounted Valuation

Incyte Corporation (INCY) is a $19 billion market cap biotech company that offers a diverse pipeline of therapies for cancer, blood disorders, and dermatology. What's notable for this biotech space is that INCY is a profitable player, and its earnings have more than doubled since 2021, based on Seeking Alpha data. I noticed the stock when Steven Cress published his top-10 picks for 2026, and I knew little about the stock before that list came out. After my own research, which I tried to make unbiased, I see that INCY indeed looks quite promising because the market seems to largely discount its pipeline and upcoming earnings inflection from IAI ("Inflammation and Autoimmunity") and oncology (Monjuvi and Zynyz) offerings. I think INCY can build on the operating leverage it has already achieved, based on the latest financial updates, with top-line-driving catalysts in 2026-2028, thus proving current Wall Street conservative estimates wrong. The stock is valued too cheaply because of that, in my view, and that's why a "Buy" rating looks like a logical thing at the current stock prices.

Why Do I Think So?

INCY operates through 3 franchises:

  1. Hematology - the central identity of the firm, with the main revenue driver still staying Jakafi (almost 58% of total sales), which cares for myelofibrosis (MF), polycythemia vera (PV), and graft-versus-host disease (GVHD).
  2. Inflammation and Autoimmunity (IAI) - a "JAK-anchored" dermatology franchise, with the key selling product being Opzelura (ruxolitinib cream 1.5%), which is currently the market leader for atopic dermatitis and vitiligo in the U.S.
  3. Oncology - the main focus is on colorectal, pancreatic, and ovarian cancers, with the main drugs being Monjuvi (tafasitamab) and Zynyz (retifanlimab), which are currently actively expanding.

While Jakafi remains the top-selling drug for INCY, the market has historically discounted INCY stock due to Jakafi's late 2028 patent cliff (although Jakafi's sales kept growing). In Q3 2025, Jakafi's revenues went up by 7% YoY, all organic. But on the IAI and oncology fronts, INCY made significantly better progress. IAI's main drug - Opzelura - reached $188 million in sales (+35% YoY), with the main growth driver coming from international adoption (non-US sales went up by 117% to $44 million). In Hematology, Niktimvo has managed to capture "13% of the third-line-plus GVHD market in its first nine months," per the executives' comments, leading to a QoQ sales growth of 27% in Q3, which is a lot. As a result, non-Jakafi's portfolio has doubled in terms of revenue addition for the quarter, with no signs of stopping in the foreseeable future.

And what impressed me the most in Q3 data was the margin expansion because Incyte beat the consensus EPS estimate by >38% (amid the revenue beat of almost 9%, also the strongest since mid-2022):

R&D expenses declined by over 11% YoY as the company culled low-value programs. I think they are actively moving toward greater commercialization for most of their products that remained in the R&D phase for years, so I would not expect a margin contraction to previous levels this year. The management noted on the call that they've paused development of several assets so that they can reallocate to the 14 key trials and "build the core business (ex. Jakafi) to $3-4 billion by 2030 while maintaining a 15-20% five-year CAGR":

Of course, INCY has to do a lot to achieve that above CAGR in sales. And the first drug that should help INCY do it is Ruxolitinib Extended-Release (XR), which should hit the market in mid-2026 (see their pipeline below). The XR is a defensive play for INCY because it's trying to convert 15-30% of today's Jakafi users to a convenient once-daily dosing regimen, hence protecting the firm's top line once the loss of exclusivity comes. Plus, it can be a higher-margin product for INCY because of the XR's production specifics.

Throughout 2026-2027, I believe the dermatology space is going to provide another strong revenue addition.

The Phase 3 results in hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) were quite good, with ~60% of patients achieving HiSCR50 and ~70% reporting mild or no pain by week 24, according to the press release (September 2025). Once they submit for approval in the US, Povorcitinib can hit the market in late 2026 or early 2027. Also, Opzelura is going international after filing for moderate AD in the EU, which is expected by year-end 2025. It can potentially be approved in 2H 2026, adding to INCY's revenue growth inflection point, in my understanding.

The Oncology segment has also been pivoting, so to speak, with Monjuvi Frontline DLBCL and Solid Tumor Readouts advancing quite well. Overall, the pipeline seems to be mostly in the "pivot" stage right now, with plenty of products waiting to enter their end markets relatively soon:

I believe INCY's odds of reaching 15-30% in sales CAGR by 2030 are quite high if the above drugs go through Phase 3 and get approvals eventually. The TAMs in hematology and precision oncology markets are going to reach $6.5 billion and over $200 billion by 2030-32, respectively, so Incyte can have a massive opportunity beyond Jakafi.

The real opportunity for investors lies in the divergence between what the management targets and what the consensus estimates reflect. Based on the current sales growth projection made by the Street, INCY is going to have a negative 5-year CAGR when Jakafi faces patent loss, and INCY's revenues naturally decline because of that.

I don't think these projections are fair to INCY or to the firm's recent attempts to diversify away from Jakafi dependence. I could have understood the pessimism if there were no drugs in INCY's pipeline setting up for launch in 2026-2027, but clearly, there are plenty of them. Plus, the Q3 data have already shown that the non-Jakafi portfolio is growing rapidly.

I think that INCY can reach the highest end of the current EPS consensus, which stands at $9.33, based on Seeking Alpha data. That would imply a 38% YoY growth if the FY2025 consensus comes in at $6.75. If we put just 15x on the FY2026 EPS that I've just assumed, we'd get a price target of $139.95/share. That's an upside potential of ~33% to the current price. So, I think that Steven Cress' pick looks very bullish indeed for 2026, and the fairest rating I can give is a "Buy."

Where Can I Be Wrong?

Naturally, a lot of INCY's upside potential lies in the firm's ability to deliver on the commercialization of its current drug pipeline. In other words, we should see continued success from INCY's attempts to bring its promising drugs to market at relatively high margins, which is actually a very difficult task to do. If we see Jakafi XR conversion failing, or some clinical/regulatory issues arise ahead, the projected business growth CAGR will likely be revised to the downside. The undervaluation that I've come up with today may become misleading. Assuming the lower end of the analysts' FY2026 EPS projections ($5.38) and putting a P/E of 10x on it, I get the bear case price target of $53.8/share, which is ~49% below today's price of the stock.

The Bottom Line

I admit that the execution risks are quite high, and INCY may not be as undervalued as I think, given its appreciation over the past year (in other words, a lot of the bullishness that I base my thesis on may already be priced in). But I think that in reality, the market is setting too conservative a bar for INCY's forward sales and earnings growth potential, focusing too much on Jakafi's upcoming patent cliff. My valuation model points to a 33% upside over the next 12 months or so. That's why I rate INCY as a "Buy."

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Equinox Gold: Improving Fundamentals, But That Seems Priced In (Upgrade)
data.description:
Equinox Gold overview

In May 2025, I gave Equinox Gold (EQX) a sell rating due to high debt, production costs of above $2,000 on an AISC basis, and an unconvincing track record of primarily growing share count (diluting per-share metrics).

The saying “the tide lifts all boats” hit me and my view on EQX fully. The stock has roughly doubled since my article came out.

Nonetheless, I do not regret having missed these gains, as I do not like to play a trend, in this case the higher gold price, via qualitatively weaker names like EQX, as I explained in my first analysis.

I am taking the strong rise in shares, together with the latest updates from Q3 2025 (update and presentation), as well as the sale of the Brazilian assets and yesterday’s update as an occasion to reassess the case.

My result is a hold rating, an upgrade to my previous sell, as a few aspects have improved. But I am not convinced enough for a buy or strong buy rating.

EQX stock - Solid improvements to upgrade to a hold rating

Since my analysis came out, EQX stock has, as said in the intro, a bit more than doubled. With this strong run, it has indeed left what I view to be higher-quality competitors behind it, and not even by a little.

The explanation is quickly found. A previously disadvantaged, lower-quality company with higher costs, and accordingly lower margins and results, benefits disproportionately more than a higher-quality name with better economics—on a relative basis. When income or cash flow is depressed, the percentage gain is much higher compared to a miner that already had decent margins (and higher profits) before.

This is the past. Now, let’s evaluate the latest developments to form a new opinion on EQX.

Starting with the latest fully-reported quarterly results, Equinox Gold presented strong figures and ongoing progress for Q3 2025. Gold production reached 236,382 ounces, including contributions from Castle Mountain and Valentine. The latter was acquired previously with the buyout of Calibre Mining.

Gold sold totaled 239,311 ounces, almost on par with produced ounces, at a realized price of $3,397 per ounce. This might sound low, but keep in mind that gold closed the third quarter a smidgen below $3,900 which was a record high at that time.

Digging deeper, total cash costs were $1,434 per ounce, with the more important all-in sustaining costs at $1,833 per ounce. This is below the level at the time of my previous analysis, and also below the YTD number of $1,904, signaling operational improvement and a strong focus on cost control.

However, despite the wide spread to the current gold price of ~$4,500, this is not a world-class level, which is closer to $1,500 per ounce and below. This is my personal point of reference.

Anyway, thanks to a strong yellow precious metal, income from mine operations was $280.1 million. Net income stood at $85.6 million, or $0.11 per share.

The reasons for this slim bottom-line are high taxes and especially exploded costs of debt. I negatively pointed out before the high absolute debt level, but interest expenses, as can be seen in the following chart, have truly eaten much of the operating profits. But this will change dramatically, as I will show further below.

Cash and equivalents ended the Q3 quarter at $348.5 million with net debt of $1.100 billion.

This was the third quarter.

Equinox Gold announced in December great news for shareholders, namely the sale of its Brazil operations to a CMOC Group subsidiary for total consideration of $1.015 billion. This is split into a $900 million upfront cash component and up to $115 million in contingent payments based on production thresholds.

The deal covers the Aurizona Mine, RDM Mine, and Bahia Complex. EQX already hinted at potential divestitures to streamline its portfolio in its Q3 presentation. Brazilian operations at that time indeed were the second-biggest block of assets, measured by their NAVs, after Canada. So this is definitely a big and transformative deal, in many senses.

Closing is expected in Q1 2026, subject to approvals. The post-sale EQX will be a smaller, but financially stronger, and more profitable company. Proceeds are expected to quickly repay $800 million in debt and reduce the revolving credit facility as well as interest expenses meaningfully. This in turn will have a dramatically positive impact on the bottom line, together with the much higher realized gold price that cracked through $4,000 during the fourth quarter, and even $4,500 recently.

A last look into the Q3 presentation, and we see that Brazilian mines accounted for about a third of previously expected total production, stressing the true size of this sale. And, more importantly, these were the absolute cost leaders in a negative sense, with AISC way north of $2,000.

The sale simplifies and right-sizes the portfolio to a smaller, lower-cost entity, shifting the focus to North American growth opportunities.

Which leads us to the just-published Q4 2025 and full 2026 preview.

EQX achieved record Q4 2025 gold production of 247,024 ounces (still including the to-be sold assets). Full-year 2025 production reached a record 922,827 ounces, exceeding guidance of 750,000–915,000 ounces modestly. All-in sustaining costs for 2025 are expected at the upper end of $1,800–$1,900 per ounce, but below $2,000.

Year-end cash and equivalents rose 24% to $430 million after $75 million in debt repayment and $70 million in tax settlements. As hinted at above, these two factors will have a massively positive impact on profits. Total debt reduction since late Q3 was $214 million with the plans post Brazil sale described above. In other words, EQX will reach almost debt-neutral territory on a net level, clearly a significant improvement.

The key mine (growth driver), Valentine, achieved commercial production on 18 November 2025, a bit ahead of schedule, with Q4 throughput at 90% of capacity.

For 2026, excluding Brazil post-sale, consolidated production guidance is 700,000–800,000 ounces, reflecting an 80% increase in Canadian output, driven by the ramp-up of Valentine. AISC is expected to improve mildly to a range of $1,775–$1,875.

Which leads us to the final question: how I am viewing the stock. With Q3 2025 numbers, EQX trades at a price to tangible book value of 2.1x.

Other investors and analysts might see it differently, but in my view—to keep things simple—this metric is a good yardstick. It is not perfect, but no valuation approach is.

What we see above is that the time of big bargains is over. EQX traded at bargain territory with a big discount to its TBV for quite some time. Now, it is at a premium. In my view, the discount before was well deserved, due to comparatively high production costs, high debt, and a historical track record of high dilution. The exploding gold price proved me wrong, but EQX was a riskier stock than higher-quality names in my view.

The setup has noticeably improved. It cannot be denied that the gold price was highly supportive in this regard. That’s why I am not giving a sell rating anymore, assuming the gold price doesn’t drop meaningfully. If this proves to be true, EQX will strongly boost its results.

With the final Q4 and FY 2025 results, the price to TBV will likely not have improved that much. But after the closing of the sale of Brazilian assets and the subsequent debt pay down, the book value will rise substantially, lowering the valuation multiple again (assumption based on the current share and gold price), likely well below 2x the TBV.

However, the reason why I upgrade only to a hold rating is that the gold price has already risen substantially. Could it go higher? Yes, absolutely. I can imagine that. But we have already seen a substantial lift, and mining equities followed suit. EQX trades at a premium, likely even post-debt pay down. Accordingly, more growth and future success are priced in and expected. As EQX remains a miner with relatively high production costs, even after the asset sale, it will also be among those to be hit the hardest, once the gold price decides to enter a correction.

In lack of a decent-enough margin of safety, I am accordingly giving a hold rating. My sell was too pessimistic. But I am reluctant to jump in now.

Risks to my Thesis

The biggest risk, in my view, is a prolonged higher gold price that could even reach higher levels. This would benefit EQX.

EQX, with its $11.5 billion market cap, could be acquired by a bigger competitor who is interested in North American assets. This would send the stock higher, if a decent premium is paid.

And finally, ongoing operating improvements could lower costs, positively benefiting the bottom line together with a lower cost of debt.

Conclusion

My new rating for EQX is hold, an upgrade from my previous sell. I acknowledge certain improvements and welcome the right-sizing through selling off the Brazilian assets to pay down debt. But production costs remain relatively high, and the stock trades at a premium, in my opinion, not enough to justify a better rating.
